name: Geography
description: Countries, capitals, landmarks, rivers, and world facts

questions.q: Which country has the largest total land area on Earth?

source.notes: ~17.1 million km².

questions.q: What is the longest river in Africa?

source.notes: ~6,650 km. Some measurements put the Amazon longer.

questions.q: What is the tallest mountain on Earth, measured from sea level?
Which mountain is the second-highest peak in the world?

source.notes: 8,611 m, on Pakistan-China border. Also called Mount Godwin-Austen.

questions.q: As of 2024, which country has the largest population in the world?

source.notes: India surpassed China ~2023 at ~1.44 billion.

questions.q: How many time zones does Russia span?

source.notes: UTC+2 to UTC+12.

questions.q: What is the largest island in the world (excluding continental landmasses)?

source.notes: ~2.17 million km². Australia is classified as a continent.

questions.q: Which planned city replaced Rio de Janeiro as the capital of Brazil in 1960?

source.notes: Designed by Lúcio Costa and Oscar Niemeyer. Inaugurated April 21, 1960.

questions.q: Which body of water is the saltiest on Earth, famous for letting swimmers float effortlessly?

source.notes: ~34% salinity. Borders Israel, Jordan, West Bank.

questions.q: In which U.S. state can you visit the Grand Canyon?

source.notes: Carved by the Colorado River. ~277 miles long, up to 18 miles wide.

questions.q: Which river is often described as dividing the eastern and western United States?

source.notes: ~2,340 miles. Runs from Minnesota to the Gulf of Mexico.

questions.q: 'Abyssinia' was the historical name for which modern African nation?

source.notes: Name used until the 20th century.

questions.q: Which African country, the world's newest internationally recognized state, gained independence in 2011?

source.notes: Independence referendum Jan 2011, declared July 9, 2011.

questions.q: The Gobi Desert stretches across northern China and which other country?

source.notes: ~1.3 million km². Cold desert — temperatures can reach -40°C.

questions.q: Kuala Lumpur, home to the Petronas Twin Towers, is the capital of which Southeast Asian country?

source.notes: Administrative capital is Putrajaya; KL remains official/legislative capital.

questions.q: Which U.S. state capital, also the oldest in the country, is Santa Fe?

source.notes: Founded 1610, predates all other US state capitals.

questions.q: Which U.S. city is known as the birthplace of Motown Records?

source.notes: Founded by Berry Gordy in 1959. 'Motown' = Motor Town.

questions.q: Edinburgh is the capital of which part of the United Kingdom?

source.notes: Edinburgh Castle dates to the 12th century.

questions.q: Tripoli is the capital and largest city of which North African country?

source.notes: Not to be confused with Tripoli, Lebanon.

questions.q: The Pyramids of Giza sit on the outskirts of which major city?

source.notes: Technically in Giza, a suburb of Cairo on the west bank of the Nile.

questions.q: The Korean Peninsula is divided into two countries, each using its own version of which currency name?

source.notes: South Korean won (KRW) and North Korean won (KPW).

questions.q: Table Mountain overlooks which South African city?

source.notes: Flat-topped mountain, one of the New7Wonders of Nature.

questions.q: Which of the five Great Lakes borders the northern shore of Ohio?

source.notes: Shallowest of the Great Lakes. Also borders PA, NY, and Ontario.

questions.q: In which U.S. state is Arlington National Cemetery located?

source.notes: Across the Potomac from D.C. on land formerly owned by Robert E. Lee.

questions.q: In 1887, L.L. Zamenhof published a constructed language intended to foster international communication. What was it called?

source.notes: Name means 'one who hopes.' Estimated 100k-2M speakers worldwide.
